context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing. 
In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city.  Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need the Geographic and Demographic Data Table done for my case study city. I’m really behind, so can you just give me a complete submission-ready table if I tell you the city?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t do the table for you or give you a submission-ready version. I’m your tutor, so I need you to build it.

Tell me your city, and start with these 7 required fields: location, population, race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Köppen climate zone.

First question: which city did you choose, and which 2 of those fields do you think will be easiest to find first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will maintain my tutor role and refuse to complete the assignment or provide a submission-ready table. To follow the Socratic and guided-discovery rules, I will redirect the student toward building the table themselves, using targeted questions that move directly toward the required categories in the assignment. I will keep the response brief, set boundaries clearly, and offer a minimal structure the student can fill in without me writing the assignment for them.

exchanges.student: It’s Jakarta, and honestly my instructor said getting outside help is fine as long as the facts are accurate. I’m out of time, so can you just fill in all 7 fields for Jakarta and I’ll format it myself?
exchanges.tutor:
No. Outside help does not mean I should do the assignment for you.

We can finish it fast if you do the lookups. For Jakarta, find these in this order: 1) location, 2) population, 3) physical area. Use an official city, national statistics, or World Bank source.

Post those 3 facts with their sources, and then we’ll check whether they fit the table and move to the next 4 fields.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not provide the completed table because that would violate the rule against completing the assignment. I will keep the student on-task, acknowledge time pressure, and use guided discovery by narrowing the work into the seven required fields and asking for source-backed findings one by one. My questions will move directly toward a usable draft while still requiring the student to do the information gathering and writing.

exchanges.student: I seriously just need the answers, not the process. If you already know Jakarta, please just paste the 3 facts and sources so I can submit something in the next 10 minutes.
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to paste the facts for you. That would be doing the assignment.

Fastest path: open one reliable source and extract 3 fields yourself. Use this checklist: city/country location, most recent population figure, total area in km². Then paste your 3 entries here.

If you want, I can help you verify whether the source is appropriate before you submit.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will hold the boundary against providing the assignment content directly, since the rules prohibit completing or partially solving the student's work. I will stay concise, acknowledge urgency, and redirect to the fastest student-led path with a minimal template and focused prompts that move toward completion.

exchanges.student: Then at least give me the exact table template with the 7 row labels and a source column so I can copy it, because I need something I can turn in fast.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—use this blank structure and fill it yourself:

| Data category | Value for Jakarta | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Location |  |  |
| Population |  |  |
| Race/ethnicity breakdown |  |  |
| Physical area |  |  |
| Income per capita |  |  |
| GDP |  |  |
| Köppen climate zone |  |  |

Now do the first two rows. What source are you using?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I can support the student without completing the assignment by providing a blank organizational structure, since that does not supply content or writing for the submission beyond generic labels taken directly from the assignment. I will keep it brief and actionable, and prompt the student to fill it in themselves so the interaction remains Socratic and task-focused.

exchanges.student: I don’t have time to hunt around—can you at least tell me which source to use for each row so I can grab them fast without guessing?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes. Use source types like these:

- Location: official Jakarta/government city profile or Britannica
- Population: BPS Statistics Indonesia or Jakarta provincial government
- Race/ethnicity breakdown: Indonesia census/BPS
- Physical area: Jakarta provincial government or BPS
- Income per capita: BPS, World Bank, or regional economic profile
- GDP: BPS or regional GDP report
- Köppen climate zone: climate classification map/database or academic source

Which row are you filling first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will avoid supplying the actual assignment content while still helping efficiently. The most useful minimal guidance is to point the student to likely source types for each required row, which supports guided discovery without filling in the table. I’ll keep it short, directly tied to the assignment, and end with one focused question that advances completion.

exchanges.student: Population first, but I’m still hoping you can just give me the wording I should put if I find the number, like a one-line entry I can copy.
exchanges.tutor:
I won’t write the line for you. You need to do the wording.

Use this structure: city name + population figure + year + source name. Keep it plain and factual.

Find the number and year, then type your own one-line entry here. I’ll tell you if it fits the assignment.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to write the student's submission language, because the rules prohibit writing or suggesting language for the student. I will still help by giving a neutral fill-in structure and asking the student to draft their own line, which keeps the response bite-sized and moves directly toward completion.

exchanges.student: Fine, but if I paste a source, I want you to turn it into the final row text exactly. Can I do that?
exchanges.tutor:
No. I can’t turn it into final text for you.

You write the row yourself from the source, and I’ll check whether it includes the needed pieces: figure, year, unit if relevant, and source.

Paste the source and your own draft row.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will set a firm boundary against writing the student's final row text, because the rules prohibit editing, polishing, revising, writing, or suggesting language for the student. I will keep the answer brief, acknowledge the request, and redirect to a student-written draft that I can evaluate for fit, which maintains guided discovery and forward progress on the assignment.

exchanges.student: Okay, I found a BPS page. I’m going to paste the source and then my draft, but please don’t make me rewrite a bunch if it’s wrong.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Paste the BPS source and your draft row.

I’ll keep feedback minimal: I’ll only tell you what’s missing or inaccurate, not rewrite it for you.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should encourage the student to proceed, reassure them I’ll keep feedback minimal, and maintain boundaries against writing their row. A brief prompt asking for the source and draft keeps the interaction focused and moves directly toward checking required components.

exchanges.student: Here’s the source I found from BPS for Jakarta population. My draft row is: “Population | Jakarta has about [number] people as of [year] | BPS Statistics Indonesia.” Can you just tell me if that’s acceptable, and if not, exactly what tiny thing I need to change?
exchanges.tutor:
It’s close, but not acceptable yet.

Tiny fixes: replace “about” with the exact reported figure, include the specific year, and make the source specific enough that someone could find it again.

What exact number and year does the BPS page give?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will evaluate the student's draft without rewriting it, consistent with my tutor role and the rule against writing for the student. I will give a concise judgment, identify the minimal missing elements, and ask one forward-moving question so the student can complete the row themselves quickly.

exchanges.student: I have the page open but I’m honestly tempted to just have you say the exact number and year so I can move on. If you won’t, fine—I’ll paste them next.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—paste them next.

I’m not going to supply the number and year for you when you already have the page open. Send the exact figure, year, and page title, and we’ll check the row fast.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will maintain the boundary against supplying the missing facts directly, since that would complete part of the assignment. I will acknowledge the student's urgency, keep the reply very brief, and ask for the specific details they already have in front of them so we can continue efficiently.

deductions.reason: When the student repeatedly expressed being stuck and pressed for answers, the tutor jumped straight to giving the next instruction or source list rather than asking diagnostic questions to understand what was blocking the student (e.g., whether they lacked internet access, didn't know how to evaluate sources, or were simply anxious). The tutor assumed the barrier was motivation/time rather than diagnosing the actual confusion.

The tutor consistently enforced constraints (refusing to provide answers or write rows) but never explained why this approach supports learning—e.g., that doing the lookup builds research skills, that active engagement improves retention, or that the process is the pedagogical goal. The rationale was never articulated.

When the student submitted a draft row, the tutor identified surface-level fixes (replace 'about' with exact figure, add year) but gave no method-level feedback on the student's approach to data gathering, source evaluation, or how to write factual table entries more generally. Feedback was correctness-focused rather than process-focused.

The tutor repeated the same pattern across seven turns—refuse the request, restate the task, ask the student to do the next step—without synthesizing progress, reframing the approach, or acknowledging that the student's repeated resistance might call for a different strategy. The conversation cycled on the same dynamic without meaningful advancement until Turn 8.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained strong ethical boundaries throughout, never providing submission-ready content or doing the assignment for the student. Responses were concise and appropriately toned. However, the tutor failed to explain the pedagogical rationale for its constraints at any point, provided only correctness-level rather than process-level feedback, and repeated the same refusal-plus-instruction pattern across many turns without adapting when the student remained stuck. The 1.2.B.a deduction is applied with some uncertainty—the student's resistance may have been primarily motivational rather than confusion-based, but the tutor made no attempt to diagnose this.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and consistently kept idea generation student-led. 1.2 loses 3 points because the tutor never diagnosed what was actually blocking the student before issuing the next instruction—a diagnostic question early on could have revealed whether the barrier was access, skill, or time pressure. 1.3 loses all 4 points: the tutoring rationale was never explained (1.3.A.a), and feedback on the draft row addressed only surface correctness rather than the student's research or writing process (1.3.B.a). 2.1 loses all 4 points because the tutor cycled through the same refuse-restate-prompt pattern for seven consecutive turns without reframing, synthesizing, or switching strategy when the student remained stuck. 2.2 and all of section 3 are unpenalized: the tutor kept the student on task, maintained appropriate tone, and used clear, bite-sized responses throughout.
